items.prompt_md: In the bottom-up `max_depth` recursion, what does a call on `root.left` **return** to its caller?
options: The depth of the left subtree, as an integer
The left child node itself
A boolean saying whether the left subtree is balanced
Nothing — depth is tracked with a nonlocal counter instead
items.answer: The depth of the left subtree, as an integer
items.explanation_md: Each recursive call returns an integer — the height of the subtree rooted at that node — and the parent combines its two children's returned integers into its own: `1 + max(left, right)`.

items.prompt_md: What should `max_depth(None)` return, and why?
options: 0, because an empty subtree contributes no height, and the parent's `1 + max(left, right)` still works out correctly
1, because even an empty subtree counts as one level
None, because there is no node to measure
-1, to signal an error condition
items.answer: 0, because an empty subtree contributes no height, and the parent's `1 + max(left, right)` still works out correctly
items.explanation_md: A leaf's real children are `None`; if `max_depth(None)` returns 0, the leaf correctly computes `1 + max(0, 0) = 1`, which is exactly right for a single node.

items.prompt_md: In the recursive definition 'a node's depth is 1 plus the taller of its two children's depths,' which traversal order does this rely on?
options: Post-order — both children's depths must be known before the node can compute its own
Pre-order — the node's depth must be known before recursing into children
In-order — the node's depth is computed between the two recursive calls
No particular order — depth can be computed independently of the children
items.answer: Post-order — both children's depths must be known before the node can compute its own
items.explanation_md: The node's own value depends on both children's returned values, so both recursive calls must finish before the combining step — that's post-order by definition.

items.prompt_md: Why can't you compute a node's depth **top-down**, the way pre-order does a node's own work before recursing?
options: Because the depth formula `1 + max(left, right)` needs the children's results, which don't exist until after they're computed
Because top-down recursion cannot visit every node
Because depth is not defined for leaf nodes
You actually can — top-down and bottom-up give identical code for depth
items.answer: Because the depth formula `1 + max(left, right)` needs the children's results, which don't exist until after they're computed
items.explanation_md: Depth is an aggregate that combines the children's values, so it's inherently a 'children first' (post-order/bottom-up) computation, not a 'this node first' one.

items.prompt_md: In the diameter solution, what does the **returned** value from `depth(node)` represent, versus what the `nonlocal best` update captures?
options: The return value is the depth this node reports to its parent; `best` separately tracks the longest path seen through any node so far
They are the same value, just stored in two places for convenience
The return value is the diameter; `best` is unused
`best` is the depth, and the return value is the diameter
items.answer: The return value is the depth this node reports to its parent; `best` separately tracks the longest path seen through any node so far
items.explanation_md: Two different quantities are threaded through the same pass: the depth (needed by the parent to keep recursing) and the diameter-so-far (a side-effect update, not needed by the parent).

items.prompt_md: Why is the longest path *through* a node computed as `left_depth + right_depth` rather than `max(left_depth, right_depth)`?
options: Because a path through the node can use both arms — down into the left subtree and down into the right subtree — so both depths are added together
Because `max` would always return the wrong subtree's depth
Because addition is required by definition of post-order traversal
`left_depth + right_depth` and `max(left_depth, right_depth)` are always equal
items.answer: Because a path through the node can use both arms — down into the left subtree and down into the right subtree — so both depths are added together
items.explanation_md: A path passing through a node can travel down its left arm and down its right arm simultaneously, so the longest such path is the sum of the two arms' depths, not just the taller one.

items.prompt_md: What is the specific bug in computing diameter by calling a **separate** `max_depth()` function fresh at every node during another walk?
options: It redoes the same subtree's depth computation over and over, turning an O(n) piece of work into O(n) work at each of up to O(n) nodes
It returns the wrong depth value at every node
It fails to visit leaf nodes
It only works on balanced trees
items.answer: It redoes the same subtree's depth computation over and over, turning an O(n) piece of work into O(n) work at each of up to O(n) nodes
items.explanation_md: Calling `max_depth()` fresh at every node re-walks that node's entire subtree, so the same edges get revisited many times over the whole tree — an O(n^2) trap, avoided by folding the depth computation into the same post-order pass as the diameter update.

items.prompt_md: For 'is this binary tree height-balanced' (every subtree's two children differ in height by at most 1), why is a single bottom-up pass better than computing height separately and then checking balance separately?
options: A single pass can compute each subtree's height and check its balance in the same post-order step, avoiding recomputing height for the same subtree twice
Balance can only be checked with BFS, never DFS
Height and balance are unrelated, so there's no benefit to combining them
Checking balance separately is always faster
items.answer: A single pass can compute each subtree's height and check its balance in the same post-order step, avoiding recomputing height for the same subtree twice
items.explanation_md: Just like diameter, a naive 'compute height, then separately check balance at every node' approach repeats the same subtree work — folding both into one bottom-up pass keeps it at O(n).

items.prompt_md: A student writes `return 1 + max(left, right)` but forgets the base case for `root is None`. What goes wrong?
options: The recursion never terminates cleanly at the leaves' `None` children, since there's no return value defined for an empty subtree, causing an error or infinite recursion
The function still works, just one level shallower
The function returns 0 for every tree
Nothing goes wrong, since `None` subtrees are automatically skipped
items.answer: The recursion never terminates cleanly at the leaves' `None` children, since there's no return value defined for an empty subtree, causing an error or infinite recursion
items.explanation_md: Without `if root is None: return 0` (or similar), the function has no defined behavior when called on a leaf's `None` child, so the recursion can't bottom out correctly.

items.prompt_md: Which of these problems is naturally solved with bottom-up aggregate recursion (a value returned per node, combined by the parent), rather than a plain per-node transform?
options: Sum of all values in every subtree, reported for the whole tree
Doubling every node's value in place
Printing each node's value as you visit it
Swapping every node's left and right children
items.answer: Sum of all values in every subtree, reported for the whole tree
items.explanation_md: A subtree sum is 'this node's value plus its children's subtree sums' — a genuine aggregate combined from the children's returned values, unlike a simple visit-and-transform.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the one-pass diameter solution that computes depth and updates the running best diameter in the same post-order walk?
options: O(n) — every node is visited once and does O(1) combining work
O(n^2) — depth must be recomputed at every node
O(n log n) — the recursion behaves like a balanced merge
O(h) — only the path down to the deepest leaf matters
items.answer: O(n) — every node is visited once and does O(1) combining work
items.explanation_md: Folding the depth return value and the diameter side-effect update into one post-order pass means each node is visited exactly once — O(n).

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of bottom-up recursion like `max_depth` or `diameter`, and what does it depend on?
options: O(h) for the recursion stack, where h is the tree's height
O(n), because every node's return value must be stored simultaneously
O(1), because only integers are returned
O(log n) always, regardless of the tree's shape
items.answer: O(h) for the recursion stack, where h is the tree's height
items.explanation_md: Like any DFS recursion, the call stack holds at most one frame per level of depth — O(h), which is O(log n) balanced but O(n) for a skewed tree.

items.prompt_md: You need the height of a binary tree — the number of edges (or nodes) on the longest root-to-leaf path — as a single number. Which approach fits?
options: Bottom-up post-order recursion returning `1 + max(left_height, right_height)` at each node
BFS with a queue, snapshotting the count of nodes on each level
A hash map keyed by node value
Two pointers converging from the tree's shallowest and deepest leaves
items.answer: Bottom-up post-order recursion returning `1 + max(left_height, right_height)` at each node
items.explanation_md: Height is a single aggregate number defined recursively in terms of the same number on both children — the bottom-up aggregation recipe.

items.prompt_md: You need the diameter of a binary tree — the longest path between any two nodes, which may or may not pass through the root — computed in one pass. Which approach fits?
options: A single post-order pass that returns each node's depth while updating a running best of `left_depth + right_depth`
BFS with a queue, tracking the widest level
Calling a separate depth-finding function fresh at every node during a second walk
A hash map keyed by depth, storing the widest bucket
items.answer: A single post-order pass that returns each node's depth while updating a running best of `left_depth + right_depth`
items.explanation_md: Diameter needs both a returned depth value and a side-effect running maximum in the same recursive step — the hallmark bottom-up aggregate pattern, done in one pass to stay O(n).

items.prompt_md: You need a yes/no answer: is every subtree of this binary tree height-balanced (its two children's heights never differ by more than 1)? Which approach fits?
options: Bottom-up recursion returning each subtree's height (or a sentinel) while checking the balance condition at each step
BFS with a queue, comparing the first and last node of each level
A hash map keyed by node value to detect imbalance
Pre-order DFS, checking balance before recursing into children
items.answer: Bottom-up recursion returning each subtree's height (or a sentinel) while checking the balance condition at each step
items.explanation_md: Balance is a per-subtree aggregate that depends on both children's heights, so it's computed bottom-up, combining the children's returned heights at each node.

items.prompt_md: You need the sum of all node values within each subtree, reported as one number per subtree, built from the leaves upward. Which approach fits?
options: Post-order recursion where each node returns its own value plus its children's returned subtree sums
Pre-order recursion that passes a running sum down from the root
BFS with a queue, summing one level at a time
An explicit stack pushing node values without combining them
items.answer: Post-order recursion where each node returns its own value plus its children's returned subtree sums
items.explanation_md: A subtree sum is defined in terms of the same quantity on both children plus the node's own value — bottom-up aggregation, built from the leaves up.

items.prompt_md: You want the running maximum of a fixed-size window as it slides one element at a time across an array. Which approach fits?
options: A monotonic deque that drops smaller elements from the back as the window slides
Bottom-up aggregate recursion over the array treated as a tree
BFS with a queue, one array segment at a time
A hash map keyed by window start index
items.answer: A monotonic deque that drops smaller elements from the back as the window slides
items.explanation_md: There's no tree here — a sliding maximum over an array is solved with a monotonic deque, a stack-family structure, not any kind of tree recursion.

items.prompt_md: You need to detect whether a singly linked list contains a cycle. Which approach fits?
options: Two pointers moving at different speeds through the list, checking if they ever meet
Bottom-up aggregate recursion returning a boolean per node
BFS with a queue, tracking nodes seen per level
A hash map keyed by node depth
items.answer: Two pointers moving at different speeds through the list, checking if they ever meet
items.explanation_md: A linked list has no branching subtrees to aggregate over — cycle detection is the classic fast/slow pointer linked-list technique.

items.prompt_md: You need the number of nodes in each subtree of a binary tree, as one integer per subtree. Which approach fits?
options: Post-order recursion returning `1 + left_count + right_count` at each node
BFS with a queue, counting nodes discovered per level
Pre-order recursion, incrementing a running counter before recursing
A hash map keyed by node value, counting duplicates
items.answer: Post-order recursion returning `1 + left_count + right_count` at each node
items.explanation_md: Subtree node-count is defined recursively from both children's counts — a direct bottom-up aggregation, the same recipe as height or subtree sum.

items.prompt_md: You need to check if two integer arrays are permutations of each other (same elements, possibly different order). Which approach fits?
options: A hash map tallying each array's element frequencies and comparing the two tallies
Bottom-up aggregate recursion treating the arrays as a tree
BFS with a queue, comparing one array segment at a time
Two pointers, one on each array, moving only inward
items.answer: A hash map tallying each array's element frequencies and comparing the two tallies
items.explanation_md: This is a flat-array frequency comparison — the hash map / frequency-count pattern, not a tree aggregation problem.

items.prompt_md: You need the maximum value found anywhere in a binary tree, as a single number combining every subtree's contribution. Which approach fits?
options: Post-order recursion returning `max(node.val, left_max, right_max)` at each node
BFS with a queue, tracking the largest node seen per level only
A hash map keyed by node value, tracking the largest key inserted
Two pointers scanning the tree's leaves from both ends
items.answer: Post-order recursion returning `max(node.val, left_max, right_max)` at each node
items.explanation_md: The maximum over a whole subtree is a combination of the node's own value and both children's returned maxima — the bottom-up aggregation recipe, not a level-by-level or lookup-based one.

items.prompt_md: You need to determine whether one binary tree is a mirror image of another, comparing whole subtree structures against each other. Which approach fits?
options: Recursion that returns a boolean by comparing each pair of corresponding nodes and recursively comparing their (swapped) children
BFS with a queue, comparing only the last node of each level
A hash map keyed by node value, checking set equality
Two pointers scanning each tree's in-order output independently
items.answer: Recursion that returns a boolean by comparing each pair of corresponding nodes and recursively comparing their (swapped) children
items.explanation_md: 'Is this whole subtree structurally a mirror of that one' is an aggregate boolean built from the children's returned comparisons — the same bottom-up recipe as height or balance.

items.prompt_md: You need to find the k-th largest element in an unsorted array without fully sorting it. Which approach fits?
options: A heap (or quickselect) that tracks the k largest values seen so far
Bottom-up aggregate recursion over the array treated as a tree
BFS with a queue, processing array segments level by level
An explicit stack pushing every array element
items.answer: A heap (or quickselect) that tracks the k largest values seen so far
items.explanation_md: This is a flat-array top-k selection problem with no tree structure to aggregate over — it's solved with a heap or partition-based selection, not tree recursion.

items.prompt_md: You need, for every node in a binary tree, both its height AND whether its subtree is a valid binary search tree — reporting one combined result per node in a single traversal. Which approach fits?
options: Post-order recursion where each call returns a small bundle (e.g. height plus min/max range) combining both children's bundles
BFS with a queue, checking BST validity only at the last level
A hash map keyed by node value, checking for duplicates
Two pointers scanning the tree's leftmost and rightmost leaves
items.answer: Post-order recursion where each call returns a small bundle (e.g. height plus min/max range) combining both children's bundles
items.explanation_md: When a node's answer depends on more than one combined fact about its children, bottom-up recursion just returns a richer bundle (a tuple or small object) instead of a single number — same recipe, more fields.
